title: Research Bits: July 14
gist: A roundup of three early-stage research results: a Northwestern/UIC energy-efficient cerebellum-inspired memtransistor for anomaly detection, a compostable fungal-mycelium PCB alternative from German researchers, and a solvent-polarity method for tunable doping of organic semiconductors from Hanyang/Oxford.

insights.claim: Researchers from Northwestern University and University of Illinois at Chicago built a molybdenum-disulfide-based memtransistor inspired by the cerebellum that ignores expected/repeated inputs and only responds strongly to unexpected events, cutting the energy cost of continuous AI monitoring versus conventional always-on pattern-recognition AI.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: Energy-efficient anomaly-only neuromorphic sensing is a differentiated approach to edge-AI power constraints; worth tracking as a licensing or acquisition target for wearable/medical-sensor chip vendors facing battery-life limits.
insights.quote: Today’s AI is remarkably good at recognizing patterns, but it often spends enormous amounts of computing power to continuously analyze streams of data—even when nothing has changed.

insights.claim: In tests on electrocardiogram recordings with both normal rhythms and arrhythmias, the cerebellum-inspired memtransistor detected an irregular heartbeat within a fraction of a second — before the heartbeat even ended — more than twice as fast as conventional AI, positioning it for wearable heart-monitor applications.
insights.speaker: Mark Hersam (quoted by Jesse Allen)
insights.so_what: A concrete performance claim (2x faster arrhythmia detection) gives medtech/wearables investors a specific benchmark to watch for follow-on commercialization or licensing deals.
insights.quote: Our cerebellum-inspired memtransistor detected an irregular heartbeat within a fraction of a second, before the heartbeat even ended. That is more than twice as fast as conventional AI.

insights.claim: Researchers from TU Bergakademie Freiberg and University of Stuttgart developed a compostable PCB substitute made from Aspergillus niger fungal mycelium — a byproduct of industrial citric acid production — that is fully biodegradable and dissolves safely in water at end of life, with electrical properties still below standard PCBs but sufficient for low-frequency prototype applications like environmental sensors, consumer goods, and toys.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: A niche but real materials-science signal on ESG-driven electronics substrates; not yet commercially competitive but worth monitoring for low-frequency IoT sensor and disposable-electronics applications where sustainability is a purchasing criterion.
insights.quote: Although the electrical properties are still below those of standard PCBs, fungal mycelium is sufficient for prototype or low-frequency applications – such as environmental sensors, consumer goods, and toys.

insights.claim: The fungal-mycelium PCB has up to 56% lower CO2 footprint than a conventional circuit board and is made from an industrial waste product without additional fossil raw materials, while still supporting molded plates with density comparable to conventional PCBs (~1.23 g/cm3) and functional component deposition via direct ink writing or standard etching.
insights.speaker: Linus Stegbauer (quoted by Jesse Allen)
insights.so_what: A quantified CO2-reduction figure (56%) gives ESG-focused electronics manufacturers and materials investors a concrete comparison point, though the technology remains far from mainstream PCB volumes.
insights.quote: In comparison to a conventional circuit board, fungal mycelium has up to 56% lower CO2 footprint and can be easily and safely dissolved in water at the end of its life.
insights.locator: section 'Fungal mycelium PCB', Linus Stegbauer quote

insights.claim: Hanyang University and University of Oxford researchers found that solvent polarity governs the reactivity of a Lewis-paired dopant (DDQ and BCF) during processing: in highly polar solvents BCF gets captured by solvent molecules and never pairs with DDQ, while in moderately polar solvents like ethyl acetate the capture is short-lived and BCF is released as the solvent evaporates, enabling fine-tuned doping without damaging the semiconductor film.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: A processing-solvent-based doping control method avoids designing entirely new dopant molecules, potentially lowering R&D cost for organic thermoelectric and flexible-electronics developers targeting wearables and self-powered sensors.
insights.quote: Our simple solvent-mediated strategy provides a new way to optimize semiconductor doping without designing entirely new dopant molecules.

insights.claim: Using ethyl acetate as the processing solvent, the Hanyang/Oxford team achieved finely tunable doping across multiple organic semiconductors, producing materials with a high thermoelectric power factor and Seebeck coefficient — metrics relevant to self-powered wearable devices and low-power sensors.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: Improved organic thermoelectric performance could feed into self-powered/energy-harvesting sensor product roadmaps, a small but growing edge-IoT materials niche.
